name: Clearly
classification_notes: Clearly uses flared terminals and high contrast derived from Old-style and Victorian serif traditions, placing it firmly in the serif category with a display-oriented, flared subcategory rather than a conventional bracketed or unbracketed serif.
summary: A flared display serif by Brian Dove spanning seven weights, drawing on Old-style and Victorian era sources for book covers, logotypes and headline work.
description: Built around condensed letterforms with soft curled details on some terminals and sharp points on others, Clearly occupies a tonal middle ground between warmth and severity. Contrast increases through the darker weights, giving the heavier cuts a more commanding presence suited to large-scale settings. OpenType features include alternate R forms, curly terminal variants for C, G, S and s, case-sensitive forms, and both lining and oldstyle figures.
foundry_blurb: Big Fog Foundry describe Clearly as a flared typeface for display and headline use, with a tone that sits between moods: soft curled forms bring warmth while sharp terminals provide counterbalance. Originally released as a single Light weight in 2022, it expanded to a full family of seven weights from Light to Black in 2025. Inspired by Old-style and Victorian era serifs and their modern interpretations, they suggest it for book covers, logotypes, wrought iron gates and funeral home branding.

name: Discotack
classification_notes: Discotack inverts the conventional stress of a slab serif, placing thick strokes on the horizontals rather than the verticals, in the tradition of reverse contrast or Tuscan display types. Its organic, flowing forms set it apart from the angular genre norm.
summary: A reverse contrast slab serif by Brian Dove that fuses disco-era lettering with a loose, organic Western sensibility, inspired by Benguiat's Discotheque (1967).
description: Where most reverse contrast display types rely on rigid geometry, Discotack introduces flowing, irregular strokes that give the letterforms an off-kilter, handmade quality. Capitals set together produce near-flat text bands top and bottom, while the lowercase introduces a wavy, undulating texture suited to playful settings. Aimed squarely at logos, large-scale display use and any context that calls for theatrical personality over neutrality.
foundry_blurb: Big Fog Foundry describe Discotack as a reverse contrast slab serif with organic, flowing strokes and a big personality, initially inspired by Benguiat's Discotheque from 1967. They position it as a Disco Cowboy's fever dream, recommending all-caps settings for flat text bands and lowercase for a wavy texture, with suggested uses including logos, belt buckles, cattle brands and kick drum decals. Licensing is available through Blaze Type.

name: Gayot New
classification_notes: A revival of a 1970s dry-transfer lettering face with an explicitly decorative, headline-only character; its futuristic yet retro tone and short-descender options place it firmly in the display category with a retro-futurist flavour.
summary: A contemporary eight-weight revival of Jean-Pierre Gayot's 1973 Mecanorma dry-transfer lettering face, extended from its original single Black weight for headline and logo use.
description: Brian Dove's reworking of the Mecanorma original retains the source material's odd mix of retro and futuristic character while adding seven new weights from Thin to Black. OpenType alternates offer a no-descender option, alternate letterforms for a, c, G and Y, and case-sensitive punctuation, giving designers room to push the face further for short-burst display settings. Best suited to logos, call-outs and large-scale headlines where its slightly awkward charm reads as personality rather than accident.
foundry_blurb: Big Fog Foundry describe Gayot New as a contemporary interpretation of Jean-Pierre Gayot's 1970s typeface, originally released as a dry-transfer lettering winner in Mecanorma's 1973 International Type-Style Contest. Designed and produced by Brian Dove, it expands the original single Black weight into a family of eight, from Thin to Black, with version 2.0 released in April 2025. They characterise its tone as 'sorta futuristic, sorta retro, a little bit ugly, and a whole lot of fun,' recommending it for logos, call-outs, short headlines and names on football jerseys or budget space travel vessels.

name: Rube
classification_notes: A single-weight unicase display face with chunky, nearly square letterforms designed explicitly for large-scale, short-burst settings such as logotypes and packaging. Its exaggerated, cartoon-inflected forms place it firmly in the display category with no conventional uppercase/lowercase distinction.
summary: A unicase, single-weight display face with blocky, nearly square letterforms suited to logotypes, packaging and comic-inflected headline work.
description: Designed by Brian Dove and released in April 2025, it comes in one chonky style with forms that sit somewhere between a rubber stamp and a cartoon prop. The unicase design collapses the distinction between capitals and lowercase into a single unified register, making it well-suited to stacked wordmarks, candy packaging and onomatopoeic sound-effect text. Latin Extended coverage supports a broad range of Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Big Fog Foundry describe Rube as a unicase typeface in a single, chonky style, simultaneously loose and blocky, with nearly square letterforms ready to be stacked, smooshed and squashed. They suggest it was sculpted from Play-Doh and probably makes a cartoon honk sound when squeezed, recommending it for logotypes, candy packaging, comic book sound effects and motivational posters, best set big and in short bursts.
